content.decision_question: Should reform prioritize the structurally deepest intervention available even at high legal/political risk, or a shallower, more deliverable intervention that leaves the core selection mechanism in place?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can clarify the comparative magnitude of inequality associated with early versus late tracking (Finland, Poland) and can document the legal authority thresholds and veto-player access documented for Hungary. It cannot resolve whether the value of durable structural equity gains should outweigh the value of political stability, legal certainty, or institutional continuity — this is a value trade-off, not a fact to be discovered.
content.tension: Deeper structural reform (delaying tracking to age 14+) most directly targets the documented driver of inequality — early selection age — but requires primary legislative change, confronts the constitutional free-choice guarantee, and faces the strongest coalition of veto players (gimnázium directors, ideological governing coalition). Shallower reforms (admissions tweaks, capacity caps) are more politically and legally deliverable but leave the structural mechanism intact.
content.title: Structural depth versus institutional friction and legal risk
